From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Stable dispersion of polymer microparticles in an aqueous medium

This article is about the polymer. For the document preparation system and markup language, see [LaTeX](/wiki/LaTeX "LaTeX"). For other uses, see [Latex (disambiguation)](/wiki/Latex_(disambiguation) "Latex (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Latex-production.jpg/250px-Latex-production.jpg)](/wiki/File:Latex-production.jpg)

[Tapping](/wiki/Rubber_tapping "Rubber tapping") of latex from *[Hevea brasiliensis](/wiki/Hevea_brasiliensis "Hevea brasiliensis")*, for use in [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber") production

**Latex** (pl. **latices**) is an [emulsion](/wiki/Emulsion "Emulsion") (stable [dispersion](/wiki/Dispersion_(chemistry) "Dispersion (chemistry)")) of [polymer](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") [microparticles](/wiki/Microparticle "Microparticle") in water.[[1]](#cite_note-1): 204–205 Latices are found in [nature](/wiki/Nature "Nature"), but synthetic latices are common as well.

In nature, latex is found as a [milky](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/milky "wikt:milky") [fluid](/wiki/Fluid "Fluid"), which is present in 10% of all [flowering plants](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") (angiosperms)[[2]](#cite_note-Agrawal-2) and in some [mushrooms](/wiki/Mushroom "Mushroom") (especially species of *[Lactarius](/wiki/Lactarius "Lactarius")*).[[3]](#cite_note-3) It is a complex emulsion that [coagulates](/wiki/Coagulation "Coagulation") on exposure to air, consisting of [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid"), [starches](/wiki/Starch "Starch"), [sugars](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar"), [oils](/wiki/Vegetable_oil "Vegetable oil"), [tannins](/wiki/Tannin "Tannin"), [resins](/wiki/Resin "Resin"), and [gums](/wiki/Natural_gum "Natural gum"). It is usually exuded after tissue injury. In most plants, latex is white, but some have yellow, orange, or scarlet latex. The fungus *[Lactarius indigo](/wiki/Lactarius_indigo "Lactarius indigo")* has a vivid blue latex. Since the 17th century, latex has been used as a term for the fluid substance in plants, deriving from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word for 'liquid'.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) It serves mainly as [defense](/wiki/Antipredator_adaptation "Antipredator adaptation") against [herbivores](/wiki/Herbivore "Herbivore")[[2]](#cite_note-Agrawal-2) and [fungivores](/wiki/Fungivore "Fungivore").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

Latex is not to be confused with [plant sap](/wiki/Plant_sap "Plant sap"); it is a distinct substance, separately produced, and with different functions. The word *latex* is also used to refer to natural latex [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber"), particularly non-[vulcanized](/wiki/Vulcanization "Vulcanization") rubber. Such is the case in products like latex [gloves](/wiki/Glove "Glove"), latex [condoms](/wiki/Condom "Condom"), [latex clothing](/wiki/Latex_clothing "Latex clothing"), and [balloons](/wiki/Balloon "Balloon").

The [IUPAC](/wiki/International_Union_of_Pure_and_Applied_Chemistry "International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry") definition of *latex* is "[colloidal](/wiki/Colloid "Colloid") dispersion of polymer particles in a liquid".[[8]](#cite_note-terminology-8) The polymer in the particles may be organic or inorganic.[[8]](#cite_note-terminology-8) The IUPAC definition of "synthetic latex" is "latex obtained as a product of an [emulsion](/wiki/Emulsion_polymerization "Emulsion polymerization"), mini-emulsion, micro-emulsion, or [dispersion polymerization](/wiki/Dispersion_polymerization "Dispersion polymerization")".[[8]](#cite_note-terminology-8)

## Biology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biology")]

### Articulated laticifers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Articulated laticifers")]

The [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") ([laticifers](/wiki/Laticifer "Laticifer")) in which latex is found make up the laticiferous system, which can form in two very different ways. In many plants, the laticiferous system is formed from rows of cells laid down in the [meristem](/wiki/Meristem "Meristem") of the [stem](/wiki/Plant_stem "Plant stem") or [root](/wiki/Root "Root"). The [cell walls](/wiki/Cell_wall "Cell wall") between these cells are dissolved so that continuous tubes, called latex vessels, are formed. Since these vessels are made of many cells, they are known as *articulated laticifers*. This method of formation is found in the [poppy family](/wiki/Papaveraceae "Papaveraceae") and in the [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber") trees ([Para rubber tree](/wiki/Hevea_brasiliensis "Hevea brasiliensis"), members of the family [Euphorbiaceae](/wiki/Euphorbiaceae "Euphorbiaceae"), members of the [mulberry and fig family](/wiki/Moraceae "Moraceae"), such as the Panama rubber tree *[Castilla elastica](/wiki/Castilla_elastica "Castilla elastica")*), and members of the family [Asteraceae](/wiki/Asteraceae "Asteraceae"). For instance, *[Parthenium argentatum](/wiki/Parthenium_argentatum "Parthenium argentatum")* the guayule plant, is in the tribe [Heliantheae](/wiki/Heliantheae "Heliantheae"); other latex-bearing Asteraceae with articulated laticifers include members of the [Cichorieae](/wiki/Cichorieae "Cichorieae"), a [clade](/wiki/Clade "Clade") whose members produce latex, some of them in commercially interesting amounts. This includes *[Taraxacum kok-saghyz](/wiki/Taraxacum_kok-saghyz "Taraxacum kok-saghyz")*, a species cultivated for latex production.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Non-articulated laticifers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Non-articulated laticifers")]

In the [milkweed](/wiki/Asclepiadaceae "Asclepiadaceae") and [spurge](/wiki/Euphorbiaceae "Euphorbiaceae") families, on the other hand, the laticiferous system is formed quite differently. Early in the development of the seedling, latex cells differentiate, and as the plant grows these latex cells grow into a branching system extending throughout the plant. In many [euphorbs](/wiki/Euphorbiaceae "Euphorbiaceae"), the entire structure is made from a single cell – this type of system is known as a *non-articulated laticifer*, to distinguish it from the multi-cellular structures discussed above. In the mature plant, the entire laticiferous system is descended from a single cell or group of cells present in the [embryo](/wiki/Embryo "Embryo").

The laticiferous system is present in all parts of the mature plant, including roots, stems, [leaves](/wiki/Leaf "Leaf"), and sometimes the [fruits](/wiki/Fruit "Fruit"). It is particularly noticeable in the [cortical](/wiki/Cortex_(botany) "Cortex (botany)") tissues. Latex is usually exuded as a white liquid, but is some cases it can be clear, yellow or red, as in [Cannabaceae](/wiki/Cannabaceae "Cannabaceae").[[2]](#cite_note-Agrawal-2)

## Productive species

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Productive species")]

Latex is produced by 20,000 [flowering plant](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") species from over 40 [families](/wiki/Family_(biology) "Family (biology)"). These include both [dicots](/wiki/Dicotyledonous "Dicotyledonous") and [monocots](/wiki/Monocotyledonous "Monocotyledonous"). Latex has been found in 14 percent of tropical plant species, as well as six percent of temperate plant species.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Several members of the fungal kingdom also produce latex upon injury, such as *[Lactarius deliciosus](/wiki/Lactarius_deliciosus "Lactarius deliciosus")* and other [milk-caps](/wiki/Lactarius_(fungus) "Lactarius (fungus)"). This suggests it is the product of [convergent evolution](/wiki/Convergent_evolution "Convergent evolution") and has been selected for on many separate occasions.[[2]](#cite_note-Agrawal-2)

## Defense function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Defense function")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Latex_being_collected_from_a_tapped_rubber_tree.jpg/250px-Latex_being_collected_from_a_tapped_rubber_tree.jpg)](/wiki/File:Latex_being_collected_from_a_tapped_rubber_tree.jpg)

[Rubber tapping](/wiki/Rubber_tapping "Rubber tapping") latex

Latex functions to protect plants from herbivores and fungi from fungivores. The idea was first proposed in 1887 by Joseph F. James, who noted that latex of [milkweed](/wiki/Asclepias_syriaca "Asclepias syriaca") "carries with it at the same time such disagreeable properties that it becomes a better protection to the plant from enemies than all the thorns, prickles, or hairs that could be provided. In this plant, so copious and so distasteful has the sap become that it serves a most important purpose in its economy".[[11]](#cite_note-11) Evidence showing this defense function include the finding that [slugs](/wiki/Slug "Slug") will eat leaves drained of their latex but not intact ones, that many insects sever the veins carrying latex before they feed, and that the latex of *[Asclepias humistrata](/wiki/Asclepias_humistrata "Asclepias humistrata")* (sandhill [milkweed](/wiki/Milkweed "Milkweed")) kills by trapping 30% of newly hatched [monarch butterfly](/wiki/Monarch_butterfly "Monarch butterfly") caterpillars.[[2]](#cite_note-Agrawal-2) This has also been found in fungi, with fewer arthropods infesting latex-producing *Lactarius* than non-latex-producing *[Russula](/wiki/Russula "Russula")*. In feeding experiments using *Lactarius*, *[Ambigolimax valentianus](/wiki/Ambigolimax_valentianus "Ambigolimax valentianus")* slugs avoid feeding on mushrooms that are exuding latex.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

Other evidence is that latex contains 50–1000× higher concentrations of defense substances than other plant tissues. These [toxins](/wiki/Toxin "Toxin") include ones that are also toxic to the plant and consist of a diverse range of chemicals that are either poisonous or "[antinutritive.](/wiki/Antinutrient "Antinutrient")"

Latex is actively moved to the area of injury; in the case of *[Cryptostegia grandiflora](/wiki/Cryptostegia_grandiflora "Cryptostegia grandiflora")*, latex more than 70 cm from the site of injury is mobilized.[[2]](#cite_note-Agrawal-2) The large hydrostatic pressure in this vine enables an extremely high flow rate of latex. In a 1935 report the botanist [Catherine M. Bangham](/w/index.php?title=Catherine_M._Bangham&action=edit&redlink=1 "Catherine M. Bangham (page does not exist)") observed that "piercing the fruit stalk of *Cryptostegia grandiflora* produced a jet of latex over a meter long, and maintained [this jet] for several seconds."[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The clotting property of latex is functional in this defense since it limits wastage and its stickiness traps insects and their mouthparts.[[2]](#cite_note-Agrawal-2)

While there exist other explanations for the existence of latex including storage and movement of plant nutrients, waste, and maintenance of water balance that "[e]ssentially none of these functions remain credible and none have any empirical support".[[2]](#cite_note-Agrawal-2)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Applications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Opium_pod_cut_to_demonstrate_fluid_extraction1.jpg/250px-Opium_pod_cut_to_demonstrate_fluid_extraction1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opium_pod_cut_to_demonstrate_fluid_extraction1.jpg)

[Opium poppy](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum") exuding fresh latex from a cut

The latex of many species can be processed to produce many materials.

* [Balatá](/wiki/Balat%C3%A1 "Balatá") and [gutta percha](/wiki/Gutta_percha "Gutta percha") latex contain an inelastic polymer related to rubber.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]
* [Chicle](/wiki/Chicle "Chicle") and [jelutong](/wiki/Dyera_costulata "Dyera costulata") tree latex was used in [chewing gum](/wiki/Chewing_gum "Chewing gum").[[14]](#cite_note-Mathews-14)

### Personal and healthcare products

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal and healthcare products")]

[Natural rubber](/wiki/Natural_rubber "Natural rubber") is the most important product obtained from latex; more than 12,000 plant species yield latex containing rubber, though in the vast majority of those species the rubber is not suitable for commercial use.[[15]](#cite_note-Bowers-15) This latex is used to make many other products including [mattresses](/wiki/Mattress "Mattress"), [gloves](/wiki/Glove "Glove"), [swim caps](/wiki/Swim_cap "Swim cap"), [condoms](/wiki/Condom "Condom"), [catheters](/wiki/Catheter "Catheter") and [balloons](/wiki/Balloon "Balloon").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Opium and opiates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Opium and opiates")]

Dried latex from the [opium poppy](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum") is called [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), the source of several useful [analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") such as [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine"), [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine"), and [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), the latter two of which can then further be used in the synthesis and manufacture of other (typically stronger) [opioids](/wiki/Opioids "Opioids") for medicinal use, and of [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") for the [illegal drug trade](/wiki/Illegal_drug_trade "Illegal drug trade"). The opium poppy is also the source of medically useful non-analgesic alkaloids, such as [papaverine](/wiki/Papaverine "Papaverine") and [noscapine](/wiki/Noscapine "Noscapine").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Clothing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Clothing")]

Main article: [Latex clothing](/wiki/Latex_clothing "Latex clothing")

Latex is used in many types of [clothing](/wiki/Clothing "Clothing"). Worn on the body (or applied directly by painting), it tends to be [skin-tight](/wiki/Skin-tight_garment "Skin-tight garment"), producing a "second skin" effect.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Coatings and glues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Coatings and glues")]

Synthetic latices are used in [coatings](/wiki/Coating "Coating") (e.g., latex paint) and [glues](/wiki/Glue "Glue") because they solidify by [coalescence](/wiki/Coalescence_(chemistry) "Coalescence (chemistry)") of the polymer particles as the water evaporates. These synthetic latices therefore can form films without releasing potentially toxic organic solvents in the environment. Other uses include cement additives and to conceal information on [scratchcards](/wiki/Scratchcard "Scratchcard"). Latex, usually [styrene](/wiki/Styrene "Styrene")-based, is also used in [immunoassays](/wiki/Immunoassay "Immunoassay").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Health and safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Health and safety")]

Main article: [Latex allergy](/wiki/Latex_allergy "Latex allergy")

Some people only experience a [mild allergy](/wiki/Allergy "Allergy") when exposed to latex, with symptoms such as [eczema](/wiki/Eczema "Eczema"), [contact dermatitis](/wiki/Contact_dermatitis "Contact dermatitis"), or developing a [rash](/wiki/Rash "Rash").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Others have a serious [latex allergy](/wiki/Latex_allergy "Latex allergy"), and exposure to latex products such as [latex gloves](/wiki/Latex_glove "Latex glove") can cause [anaphylactic shock](/wiki/Anaphylactic_shock "Anaphylactic shock"). [Guayule](/wiki/Guayule "Guayule") latex has only 2% of the levels of protein found in *[Hevea](/wiki/Hevea "Hevea")* latices, and it is being researched as a lower-allergen substitute.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Additionally, chemical processes may be employed to reduce the amount of [antigenic protein](/wiki/Antibody_generator "Antibody generator") in *Hevea* latex, yielding alternative materials such as [Vytex Natural Rubber Latex](/wiki/Vytex_Natural_Rubber_Latex "Vytex Natural Rubber Latex") which provide significantly reduced exposure to latex allergens.

About half of people with [spina bifida](/wiki/Spina_bifida "Spina bifida") are also allergic to natural latex rubber. People who have had multiple surgeries and who have had prolonged exposure to natural latex are also more susceptible to a latex allergy.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Many people with latex allergy also experience allergic reactions to certain fruits. This association has led to research regarding latex-fruit syndrome (LFS). This is a phenomenon characterized by cross-reactivity between natural latex rubber allergens and certain fruit allergens, leading to allergic reactions in sensitized individuals. It was described for the first time by Blanco et al. in 1994.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In a 2024 comprehensive review by Gromek et al., the last 30 years of research on LFS were summarized, focusing on its prevalence, common cross-reactions, and clinical manifestations. The review found that the prevalence of LFS in latex-allergic patients varies widely, ranging from 4% to 88%, depending on diagnostic methods, geographical regions, and study populations. The most commonly implicated fruits in LFS include banana, avocado, kiwifruit, and papaya. Clinical manifestations are predominantly systemic, with 73% of hypersensitivity symptoms being systemic and 27% localized. Gromek et al. also highlighted the need for standardized diagnostic criteria and severity grading systems to improve the accuracy of LFS diagnosis and treatment.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Microbial degradation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Latex&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Microbial degradation")]

Several species of the [microbe](/wiki/Microbe "Microbe") genera *[Actinomycetes](/wiki/Actinomycetota "Actinomycetota")*, *[Streptomyces](/wiki/Streptomyces "Streptomyces")*, *[Nocardia](/wiki/Nocardia "Nocardia")*, *[Micromonospora](/wiki/Micromonospora "Micromonospora")*, and *[Actinoplanes](/wiki/Actinoplanes "Actinoplanes")* are capable of consuming rubber latex.[[25]](#cite_note-25) However, the rate of biodegradation is slow, and the growth of bacteria utilizing rubber as a sole carbon source is also slow.[[26]](#cite_note-26)
